exemplar.title: To what extent does screen time affect circadian rhythm in terms of daytime alertness and sleep quality?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does screen time affect circadian rhythm in terms of daytime alertness and sleep quality?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear, concise title and effective use of headings, tables and figures to communicate the topic
Adequate explanation of background concepts (melatonin, blue light, ICT use) linking to research variables
weaknesses: Research question remains too broad and partially misaligned with a correlational design
Methodology justification is thin with limited range of methods and no discussion of confounding variables
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The criterion strata averaged in the lower half of the 3â4 band: communication and explanation were adequate (band 3-4), the research question was clear but only partially focused (band 3-4), and the methodological rationale was limited (band 1-2). An overall mark of 3 fairly reflects this middling performance.

strengths: Generally relevant application of source material with coherent summaries in the background
Accurate and consistent use of key biological and statistical terminology
weaknesses: Literature discussion is largely descriptive with minimal critical synthesis
Occasional imprecise phrasing and lack of deeper conceptual linkage to mechanisms
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands sat securely in the 3â4 band: source material application was mostly relevant but only partially effective, and terminology use was accurate but lacked the consistency and depth of the top band. A midâband score of 3 is therefore appropriate.

strengths: Calculation and presentation of descriptive statistics and Pearsonâs r for multiple data sets
Wellâannotated scatterplots and tables that facilitate comparison across groups
Some acknowledgement of methodological limitations and sample size in the discussion
weaknesses: Statistical analysis remains surface-level with no significance testing or confidence intervals
Evaluation is brief and generalised, offering limited critical interrogation of alternative explanations
Argument contains inconsistencies, e.g., implying causation from correlation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand (analysis, evaluation, argument) fell at the lower end of the 4â6 band, demonstrating adequate engagement with the research question but lacking the depth and coherence of the higher bands. An overall score of 4 reflects this level of critical thinking.

strengths: Adheres to the extended essay structure with a comprehensive table of contents, page numbering and word count
Clear section headings and consistent use of tables and figures enhance navigability
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation and referencing style, with occasional missing information
Layout issues such as redundant captions and uneven distribution of figures disrupt flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets most formal requirements (structure, formatting, labelling) and aligns with the 3â4 band, but inconsistencies and minor organisational lapses justify a mark of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Strengths:** The essay adheres to the required EE structure with an abstract-style opening, clear section headings, tables, figures and appendices. The flow from introduction to conclusion is easy to follow.

**Weaknesses:** Some structural elements are under-developed (e.g., reflections not integrated, âHypothesisâ placed outside the main argument). The analysis section is lengthy and partly descriptive, causing imbalance. Minor organisational lapses (redundant figure captions, repeated text) disrupt coherence.

**Overall:** The structure is **generally appropriate** though not consistently well balanced, aligning with the **3-4 band**; a score of **3** is assigned.

**Strengths:** A title page, table of contents, page numbering, section headings, and word count are present. Tables and figures are labelled, and a bibliography in (largely) consistent format is provided.

**Weaknesses:** Citation style shows inconsistency (mix of parenthetical citations and footnote-style references; occasional missing details). Figure quality varies; some images include redundant explanatory text that blurs layout. Minor typographical issues (e.g., double headings, extra line breaks) detract from professional appearance.

**Overall:** Most formal presentation requirements are met, but execution is **imperfect**, placing the work in the **3-4 band**; a mark of **3** is appropriate.

**Strengths:** The essay follows a logical sequence (Introduction â Method â Analysis â Discussion â Conclusion). Arguments are generally understandable and relate data to the research question. The student recognises limitations and suggests future research, indicating some reflective thinking.

**Weaknesses:** The line of argument is **intermittent and occasionally inconsistent**âfor instance, the conclusion hints at a beneficial effect of screen time for older participants despite earlier describing the correlation as negligible. Transitions between statistical findings and biological explanations are often weak, and some claims lack explicit evidential support.

**Overall:** The argument is **clear but contains gaps and inconsistencies**, fitting the lower end of the **4-6 band**; a mark of **4** is appropriate.

**Strengths:** The title is clear, concise and immediately signals the biological focus on circadian rhythm. The introduction explains key background concepts (melatonin, blue light, ICT use) and links them to the stated variables of daytime alertness and sleep quality. Headings, tables and figures help the reader grasp the scope and intent of the work.

**Limitations:** Explanations, while generally correct, remain largely *descriptive* and sometimes stray into tangential detail (e.g., biochemical pleiotropy of melatonin) without tying every element explicitly to the research focus. The causal direction implied by âimpactâ is not critically unpacked, and the candidate does not fully acknowledge that the chosen design can only test correlation. 

**Overall:** Communication is **adequate-to-good** and the topic is sufficiently explained for a reader to understand purpose and context, matching the 3-4 band; the clearer structure and mostly accurate exposition justify the upper mark of 4.

**Strengths:** The bibliography lists an adequate number of academic and popular science sources, many of which are relevant peer-reviewed articles on sleep, melatonin and screen exposure. Key ideas from these sources are summarized in the background section and occasionally invoked when interpreting findings.

**Weaknesses:** Much of the literature discussion is **descriptive**; sources are rarely critiqued, synthesised or used to build a nuanced biological framework. In the analysis section, external sources are scarcely employed to interpret statistical results, so application to the research findings is **partial rather than fully effective**.

**Overall:** The source material is mostly relevant and demonstrates competent biological knowledge, but use is uneven and sometimes superficial, fitting the **3-4 band**; a score of **3** is appropriate.

**Strengths:** The student calculates descriptive statistics and Pearsonâs r for four data sets, presents histograms and scatterplots, and comments on the meaning of positive and negative correlations. The analysis refers back to the research variables (sleep quality, daytime alertness) and distinguishes between age groups.

**Weaknesses:** The statistical treatment remains **surface-level**; no tests of significance or confidence are provided, and the limitations of self-reported categorical data for Pearsonâs r are only acknowledged post-hoc. Biological interpretation of patterns is limited, and alternative explanations (e.g., confounders such as caffeine or social factors) are not rigorously explored.

**Overall:** The analysis shows **adequate engagement and partial relevance** to the RQ but lacks depth and critical interrogation, suiting the **4-6 band** at the lower level; score **4**.

**Strengths:** The student justifies the choice of the PSQI and ESS instruments and gathers primary data via a questionnaire administered to two age groups. Basic statistical tools (mean, median, mode, Pearsonâs r, histograms, scatterplots) are outlined.

**Weaknesses:** The **range of methods is narrow**âall data stem from a single online questionnaire with a small convenience sample (n=40). Key methodological details (ethical procedures, validation of self-reported screen-time, control of confounding variables such as caffeine, exercise, light exposure) are missing. Rationale for the sampling strategy and the age-group split is thin, and there is no consideration of alternative or complementary methods (e.g., actigraphy, sleep diaries).

**Overall:** Evidence of method selection exists but is **limited and only minimally justified**, consistent with the **1-2 band**; a top-band mark of **2** is awarded.

**Strengths:** The discussion recognises the weakness of correlations, the small sample size, and methodological constraints, showing an attempt at evaluation. Some linkage to literature (e.g., UDAYA study) is made in the conclusion, indicating emerging synthesis.

**Weaknesses:** Evaluation is **brief and generalised**, often summarising rather than critically weighing evidence. Conclusions are cautious but still claim possible benefits of higher screen time for older participants without sufficient support. Alternative interpretations and the impact of uncontrolled variables are only superficially addressed.

**Overall:** The strand meets the descriptors for **adequate evaluation with partially supported conclusions** (band 4-6). Given the limited depth and coherence, a score of **4** within the band is warranted.

**Strengths:** The research question is explicitly stated, uses IB terminology ("To what extent"), identifies two measurable aspects (sleep quality, daytime alertness) and a defined population (young adults). This provides a recognizable focus for the study.

**Weaknesses:** The scope is still **rather broad**ââscreen timeâ covers multiple devices/contexts and âyoung adultsâ spans two age groups without explicit justification of their selection. The causal wording (âaffectâ) does not sit comfortably with the correlational method employed, signalling only partial conceptual alignment between question and method.

**Overall:** The question is clear but not fully delimited nor entirely aligned with feasible investigation, placing it in the **3-4 band**; a mid-band mark of **3** is appropriate.

**Strengths:** The essay makes regular use of biological terminologyâcircadian rhythm, melatonin, sleep latency, Pearsonâs correlationâgenerally accurately. Definitions for statistical terms (mean, median, mode) and physiological processes are provided.

**Weaknesses:** Occasional inaccuracies or vague phrasing appear (e.g., describing r = 0.559 as âweakâ rather than moderate; implying causation from correlation). Conceptual depth is limited; key biological mechanisms (e.g., melanopsin signalling pathways) are mentioned but not analysed in detail.

**Overall:** Terminology is **mostly accurate and appropriate** but lacks the precision and consistency required for the top band, placing it securely in the **3-4 band** with a mark of **3**.

annotations.comment: The inclusion of a word count at the start of the report meets formal IB requirements and demonstrates attention to submission guidelines.
Interpretation of the r=0.254 chart repeats language from earlier sections without advancing the evaluation or considering methodological caveats.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well formatted, allowing the reader to navigate the extended essay structure with ease.
The personal anecdote adds little scholarly justification for the topic; a more rigorous rationale would draw on literature gaps or societal importance.
The title clearly communicates the focus on circadian rhythm and identifies key variables, providing an effective introduction to the biological scope of the investigation.
The research question is clearly stated but remains too broad, covering multiple device types and a wide age range without justification, and it implies causation despite a correlational design.
Restating the research question in the introduction is redundant; instead, this section should briefly contextualize it and explain its significance to the investigation.
The histograms are described well but the interpretation does not relate back to the research question or discuss potential confounding variables, limiting critical depth.
The commentary on the r=0.231 chart reiterates its weakness but offers no discussion of potential causes or implications of this pattern.
Background information is descriptive and summarises sources without critical synthesis; the essay would benefit from integrating and critiquing studies to build a coherent theoretical framework.
The extended discussion of melatonin-related health issues drifts from the focus on screen time impacts; link each point explicitly back to the research variables to maintain coherence.
The phrase âlow imported healthâ appears imprecise; ensure accurate use of terminology (e.g., âpoor perceived healthâ) and check for OCR errors in key source descriptions.
This paragraph repeats earlier background without new insight; consider consolidating redundant content and focusing on how melatonin mechanisms relate directly to screen time effects.
The detailed explanation of melatonin pleiotropy, while scientifically accurate, lacks connection to sleep quality or alertness outcomes; tie these mechanisms explicitly to observed variables.
The hypothesis refers to teenagers, conflicting with the stated population of young adults; this mismatch undermines focus and requires alignment of terminology and scope.

Methodology justification is thin: a convenience sample of 40 lacks rationale, and no mention is made of ethics, validity of self-reported screen time, or control of confounders like caffeine or exercise.
Describing age as a controlled variable while also comparing two age groups is conceptually inconsistent; clarify which factors are held constant and justify the grouping strategy.
The planned analysis omits significance testing or confidence intervals; Pearsonâs r alone is insufficient for robust statistical inference and should be complemented by significance assessments.

The figure narration repeats descriptive details; integrate the histogram findings into analytical discussion that addresses why teenagers may sleep 5.5â6 hours in light of screen exposure literature.
The comparison of sleep patterns between age groups lists plausible influences but lacks linkage to secondary sources or evidence, making the evaluation largely speculative.
Section headings for the Pearson correlation analysis are clearly labelled and logically ordered, enhancing navigability and reader comprehension.
The use of numerous small tables in rapid succession hinders readability; consider consolidating scoring tables or using clear subheadings to improve layout coherence.
Describing r=0.559 as a âweakâ positive correlation is a misinterpretation; this value falls within a moderate range and should be accurately characterised.
Summarising Pearson coefficients in a clear table aids the reader in comparing relationships across groups and variables at a glance.
The scatter charts include well-annotated axes, trendlines and coefficient values, facilitating visual interpretation of the correlations.
The opening of the discussion restates methods and findings but lacks deeper critical evaluation linking back to literature or theory.
References follow mixed citation styles and inconsistent formatting; adopt a single, recognized style (e.g., APA or MLA) to enhance professionalism.
The first page lacks visible page numbering, which should appear consistently in the header or footer of every page to meet formatting requirements.
